learning_objectives: Understand the historical evolution of media theory from print to digital
Identify key theorists and their contributions to media studies
Analyze how media shapes and is shaped by culture
readings: Understanding Media by Marshall McLuhan, Ch. 1 (The Medium is the Message)
Media & Culture by Campbell, Martin, and Fabos, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Media Studies. This course examines how media systems operate, how audiences interact with media, and how media practitioners can create ethical, effective content across platforms.
key_concepts: McLuhan's 'The medium is the message' and technological determinism
Stuart Hall's encoding/decoding model of communication
Uses and gratifications theory: audiences as active participants
The Frankfurt School and critical media theory
Convergence culture and participatory media (Henry Jenkins)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of the same news story across print, broadcast television, Twitter/X, and TikTok to illustrate how the medium shapes the message
setup_requirements: Access to university library databases (JSTOR, Communication Abstracts)
Social media accounts for observation (personal accounts acceptable)
Note-taking setup for media diary assignment

review.value: The theory that media technology itself is the primary driver of social and cultural change, independent of content
Stuart Hall's model where producers encode meaning into media texts, but audiences decode them through dominant, negotiated, or oppositional readings

learning_objectives: Map the current digital media ecosystem including platforms, creators, and distributors
Understand platform business models and their influence on content
Analyze the shift from broadcast media to algorithmic content distribution
readings: Platform Capitalism by Nick Srnicek, Ch. 2
Algorithms of Oppression by Safiya Noble, Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: The media landscape has fundamentally shifted from a broadcast model with few creators and many consumers to a networked model where anyone can publish but platforms control distribution.
key_concepts: Platform economics: network effects, two-sided markets, data extraction
Algorithmic curation vs editorial curation and their biases
Creator economy: monetization models (ads, subscriptions, patronage, sponsorship)
Walled gardens vs open web: content portability and platform lock-in
The attention economy and its psychological implications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the distribution path of a piece of content from creation through platform algorithms to audience consumption, identifying each stakeholder's incentives
setup_requirements: Access to platform analytics examples (YouTube Studio, Instagram Insights)

review.value: The phenomenon where a platform becomes more valuable as more users join, creating a self-reinforcing cycle that leads to market dominance
A framework treating human attention as a scarce commodity that media platforms compete to capture and monetize

learning_objectives: Apply quantitative and qualitative methods to analyze media audiences
Distinguish between demographics, psychographics, and behavioral segmentation
Evaluate audience research methodologies and their limitations
readings: Audience Analysis by Denis McQuail, Ch. 3-4
Spreadable Media by Henry Jenkins, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding your audience is the difference between content that resonates and content that disappears. Today we learn the frameworks and tools for knowing who you are reaching and why.
key_concepts: Demographics (age, gender, location, income) vs psychographics (values, attitudes, lifestyle)
Behavioral segmentation: usage patterns, engagement metrics, loyalty indicators
Nielsen ratings, ComScore, and digital analytics platforms
Qualitative methods: focus groups, interviews, ethnographic observation
Audience personas and their role in content strategy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an audience persona using real anonymized data from Google Analytics and social media insights, combining quantitative metrics with qualitative interview findings

review.value: Dividing audiences by psychological characteristics including values, opinions, interests, attitudes, and lifestyles rather than observable demographics
A research-based fictional profile representing a key audience segment, including demographic details, behaviors, goals, pain points, and media consumption habits

learning_objectives: Apply narrative structure principles to different media formats
Understand transmedia storytelling and world-building strategies
Adapt a story for platform-specific constraints and audience expectations
readings: Convergence Culture by Henry Jenkins, Ch. 3 (Searching for the Origami Unicorn)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every medium has its own grammar for storytelling. A story told on TikTok differs fundamentally from the same story told as a podcast or documentary, not just in length but in structure, pacing, and emotional arc.
key_concepts: Three-act structure and its adaptation across formats (30-second reel to feature film)
Transmedia storytelling: extending narratives across multiple platforms with each adding unique value
Platform-native storytelling: vertical video, thread narratives, interactive stories
World-building: creating universes that support multiple entry points and story threads
The hero's journey in interactive and participatory media
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study of a successful transmedia franchise (Marvel Cinematic Universe) showing how story elements are distributed across films, TV series, comics, and social media while maintaining narrative coherence
setup_requirements: Access to streaming platforms for case study clips

review.value: A narrative technique where story elements are distributed across multiple media platforms, each making distinct and valuable contributions to the whole, not mere adaptations
Content created specifically for the constraints and affordances of a particular platform (e.g., vertical video for TikTok, threads for Twitter, carousels for Instagram)

class_schedule.topic: Social Media Strategy and Community Building
learning_objectives: Develop a data-informed social media strategy aligned with organizational goals
Understand community management principles and engagement tactics
Evaluate social media metrics and distinguish vanity metrics from meaningful KPIs
readings: Jab, Jab, Jab, Right Hook by Gary Vaynerchuk, Ch. 2-3
Killing Marketing by Joe Pulizzi and Robert Rose, Ch. 4

lecture_notes.introduction: Social media is not just a distribution channel -- it is a relationship engine. Effective strategy goes beyond posting schedules to building genuine community and measurable business outcomes.
key_concepts: Content pillars: defining 3-5 thematic categories that align brand voice with audience interests
Engagement rate vs reach vs impressions: what each metric actually measures
Community management: moderation policies, response time standards, crisis communication
The funnel: awareness, consideration, conversion, advocacy on social platforms
Paid vs organic strategy and the declining reach of organic content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Auditing a real brand's social media presence across platforms: calculating engagement rates, identifying content pillar distribution, and drafting a 30-day content calendar with KPI targets
setup_requirements: Social media analytics access (provided case study data)

review.value: Total engagements (likes, comments, shares, saves) divided by total followers (or reach), expressed as a percentage; more meaningful than raw follower count
Three to five recurring thematic categories that structure a brand's content strategy, ensuring consistency while providing enough variety to sustain audience interest

class_schedule.topic: Media Ethics, Intellectual Property, and Misinformation
learning_objectives: Apply ethical frameworks to media production and distribution decisions
Understand copyright, fair use, Creative Commons, and intellectual property law
Identify misinformation and disinformation tactics and evaluate countermeasures
readings: Media Ethics: Issues and Cases by Philip Patterson and Lee Wilkins, Ch. 5-6
Weaponized Lies by Daniel Levitin, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: With the power to publish comes the responsibility to do so ethically. This session examines the frameworks that guide media practitioners in making decisions about truth, ownership, and harm.
key_concepts: Ethical frameworks: utilitarianism, deontology, virtue ethics applied to media decisions
Copyright basics: exclusive rights, duration, registration, international treaties (Berne Convention)
Fair use four-factor test: purpose, nature of work, amount used, market effect
Creative Commons licenses: CC-BY, CC-BY-SA, CC-BY-NC, CC0 and their practical implications
Misinformation vs disinformation vs malinformation: definitions and identification strategies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: evaluating a real media ethics dilemma (publication of leaked documents) using multiple ethical frameworks, then auditing a piece of viral content for misinformation indicators
setup_requirements: Access to fact-checking databases (Snopes, PolitiFact, Full Fact)

review.value: US copyright doctrine evaluating: (1) purpose and character of use, (2) nature of copyrighted work, (3) amount and substantiality used, (4) effect on market value
Misinformation is false content shared without intent to harm; disinformation is deliberately crafted and spread to deceive; malinformation is genuine information shared to cause harm

quizzes.topic: Media Theory and the Digital Landscape

questions.question: Marshall McLuhan argued that 'the medium is the message.' Using a specific contemporary example, explain what this means and evaluate whether his framework still applies in the age of algorithmic content distribution.
questions.answer: McLuhan meant that the medium through which content is delivered has a greater effect on society than the content itself. For example, TikTok's short-form vertical video format has fundamentally changed how information is consumed, regardless of whether the content is educational, entertainment, or news. The 60-second constraint, algorithmic For You Page, and swipe interaction shape attention spans and expectations. This framework still applies because algorithmic distribution adds a new layer: the algorithm itself becomes part of the medium, filtering and prioritizing content in ways that shape collective understanding independently of creator intent.

rubric.key: Provides a specific, relevant contemporary example
Evaluates applicability to algorithmic distribution with reasoning

questions.question: Define the term 'network effects' in the context of platform economics and explain how network effects create barriers to entry for competing platforms.
questions.answer: Network effects occur when a product or platform becomes more valuable as more people use it. In platform economics, this creates a self-reinforcing cycle: more users attract more content creators, which attracts more users. This creates barriers to entry because a new competitor starts with zero users and zero content, making their platform less valuable than the incumbent. Users face high switching costs because their social graphs, content history, and audience are locked into the existing platform. Examples include Facebook's social graph, YouTube's creator ecosystem, and Amazon's marketplace of third-party sellers.

rubric.key: Identifies specific barriers (switching costs, lock-in) with examples

questions.question: Explain the difference between demographic and psychographic audience segmentation. A game studio is launching a narrative puzzle game. Which segmentation approach would be more useful for their marketing strategy, and why?
questions.answer: Demographic segmentation divides audiences by observable, measurable characteristics: age, gender, income, education, location. Psychographic segmentation divides audiences by psychological attributes: values, interests, attitudes, lifestyle, personality. For a narrative puzzle game, psychographic segmentation is more useful because the target audience is defined by interests (puzzle solving, story-driven experiences, intellectual challenge) and attitudes (preference for single-player, patience for complex narratives) rather than age or income. Two 35-year-old professionals may have completely different gaming preferences, but people who enjoy escape rooms and mystery novels across all demographics are likely prospects.

rubric.key: Provides concrete examples relevant to the game scenario

questions.question: Henry Jenkins defines transmedia storytelling as distinct from adaptation. What is the key difference, and provide an example of a franchise that uses transmedia storytelling effectively, explaining how at least two platforms each contribute unique narrative value.
questions.answer: The key difference is that adaptation retells the same story in a different medium (e.g., a novel adapted into a film), while transmedia storytelling distributes different story elements across platforms where each medium contributes something new and unique that could not exist on another platform. The Star Wars franchise is a strong example: the films tell the core saga, The Clone Wars animated series explores the period between Episodes II and III with character arcs (Ahsoka Tano) that would not fit in films, the video game Jedi: Fallen Order tells an original story set between Episodes III and IV where player agency creates unique narrative engagement, and novels like 'Lost Stars' provide perspective from ordinary soldiers that deepens the universe without duplicating film content.

rubric.key: Explains unique contribution of at least two platforms

major_assignments.title: Cross-Platform Media Analysis Report
major_assignments.description: Select a current media event, brand campaign, or cultural phenomenon and analyze how it operates across at least 3 media platforms. Apply media theory frameworks from the course to evaluate how platform affordances shape message, audience reception, and cultural impact.
objectives: Apply media theory to real-world content analysis
Demonstrate understanding of platform-specific affordances and constraints
Evaluate audience engagement using both quantitative metrics and qualitative analysis
Synthesize findings into a coherent analytical argument
deliverables: 3000-4000 word analytical report with proper citations (APA format)
Platform comparison matrix showing affordances, content format, audience behavior, and metrics
Audience analysis section including at least one persona based on observable data
Visual appendix with annotated screenshots from each platform

criteria: Correctly applies at least 3 media theory frameworks from course readings
Theory is integrated into analysis, not just summarized
Demonstrates understanding of framework strengths and limitations
Original analytical insights beyond surface-level observation
Thorough analysis of content on each platform
Identifies specific platform affordances shaping the content
Compares and contrasts audience engagement across platforms
Includes quantitative data where available
Audience analysis goes beyond demographics to psychographics
Engagement patterns are explained with reasoning
Persona is research-based and specific
Considers multiple audience segments and their different readings
Clear thesis and logical argument structure
Proper APA citation format throughout
Professional writing quality and tone
Visual appendix is well-organized and annotated
Sophisticated application of media theory to real-world analysis
Thorough and comparative platform analysis with data
Nuanced audience understanding demonstrating research skills
Professional writing and presentation quality

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of media theory concepts, terminology, and analytical frameworks

media_diary_and_reflections.description: Weekly media consumption journal entries with critical reflection on observed patterns

major_assignment.description: Cross-platform media analysis report demonstrating integrated analytical skills

group_presentation.description: Team presentation analyzing a media case study with Q&A

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering media theory, digital landscape, ethics, and applied analysis

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions and group presentation days. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 10% per day. Extensions may be granted with 48 hours advance notice and a valid reason.
course_policies.collaboration: Group projects require documented equal contribution. Individual assignments must be your own analysis and writing. Proper citation of all sources is required.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, fabrication of data, and submitting AI-generated text as your own analysis will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop or tablet for in-class activities, internet access for research, and social media accounts for observation exercises. No specific software purchases required.
